MTH6139 Time Series

Coursework 1 Gold Prices

Shaivi Darsi

Spring term 2025

1. Introduction

This project analyses historical daily gold prices from 1970 to 2026 and applies the Prophet model to forecast future values. Gold is widely considered a safe-haven asset, which means its price increases during periods of economic uncertainty.

The aim of this project is to explore trends in gold prices, build a forecasting model, evaluate the importance of seasonality, and compare forecasts using recent data.

2. Data Preparation

2.1 Loading the data

gold_data <- read.csv("data/gold_price_1970_2026_daily.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
str(gold_data)
## 'data.frame':    20819 obs. of  7 variables:
##  $ Date     : chr  "1/1/1970" "1/2/1970" "1/3/1970" "1/4/1970" ...
##  $ Open     : num  35.3 35.1 35.4 35.9 35.8 ...
##  $ High     : num  35.3 35.2 35.4 36.1 35.9 ...
##  $ Low      : num  35 35.1 35.3 35.8 35.7 ...
##  $ Close    : num  35.2 35.1 35.4 35.9 35.9 ...
##  $ Volume   : int  463815 118078 268897 441570 443895 369914 367899 339524 215043 332336 ...
##  $ USD_Index: num  101.7 99.6 99.4 99.7 99.9 ...
head(gold_data)
##       Date     Open     High      Low    Close Volume USD_Index
## 1 1/1/1970 35.26648 35.34705 35.02873 35.18484 463815 101.65398
## 2 1/2/1970 35.11844 35.21060 35.11303 35.14676 118078  99.60224
## 3 1/3/1970 35.39048 35.40160 35.30880 35.38576 268897  99.37285
## 4 1/4/1970 35.87716 36.12963 35.81995 35.93960 441570  99.68926
## 5 1/5/1970 35.84976 35.91335 35.72434 35.86630 443895  99.94307
## 6 1/6/1970 35.79759 35.98335 35.69822 35.79316 369914 100.01784

The dataset contains daily gold prices recorded from 1970 to 2026. In this project, we focus on the closing price. For further analysis we can also look at the open, high and low prices in this datset.

2.2 Cleaning column names

names(gold_data) <- trimws(names(gold_data))
names(gold_data) <- tolower(names(gold_data))

Column names are cleaned to remove spaces and capital letters to ensure consistency.

2.3 Converting dates

library(lubridate)
## Warning: package 'lubridate' was built under R version 4.4.3
## 
## Attaching package: 'lubridate'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     date, intersect, setdiff, union
gold_data$date <- mdy(gold_data$date)
gold_data <- gold_data[!is.na(gold_data$date), ]

The date column is converted into a proper date format that prophet can understand as this datset’s date format is slightly different. The missing values are removed as well.

2.4 Preparing data for Prophet

prophet_data <- data.frame(
  ds = gold_data$date,
  y = gold_data$close
)

Prophet requires a dataframe with:

  • ds: date column which is the daily date the price is recorded
  • y: variable to forecast which is the closing price

3. Exploratory Analysis

plot(prophet_data$ds, prophet_data$y, type = "l",
     main = "Gold Prices Over Time",
     xlab = "Year", ylab = "Price")

The plot shows a clear long term upward trend in gold prices. There are periods of volatility and sharper increases, particularly after the early 2000s, which indicates changing market conditions.

4. Model Building

4.1 Log transformation

prophet_data$y <- log(prophet_data$y)

A logarithmic transformation is applied here to stabilise variance and capture growth patterns better.

4.2 Fitting the model

library(prophet)
## Warning: package 'prophet' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Loading required package: Rcpp
## Warning: package 'Rcpp' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Loading required package: rlang
## Warning: package 'rlang' was built under R version 4.4.3
gold_model <- prophet(prophet_data)
## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.

The Prophet model is fitted using the log transformed data. Important to note it shows log transformed gold prices and not actual prices.

4.3 Forecasting

future_dates <- make_future_dataframe(gold_model, periods = 365)
gold_forecast <- predict(gold_model, future_dates)

The model forecasts gold prices one year into the future(1 year is 365 days)

5. Results

5.1 Forecast plot

plot(gold_model, gold_forecast)

This plot shows historical log-transformed prices along with the forecast. The blue line represents predicted values, while the shaded region represents uncertainty intervals. The forecast indicates continued upward growth with increasing uncertainty over time.

5.2 Model components

prophet_plot_components(gold_model, gold_forecast)
## Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0.
## ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`.
## ℹ See also `vignette("ggplot2-in-packages")` for more information.
## ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the prophet package.
##   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/facebook/prophet/issues>.
## This warning is displayed once per session.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

The components plot breaks the forecast into trend and seasonal effects:

  • The Trend (Top) component shows long-term movement of gold prices (log scale): X-axis shows the time Y-axis shows the trend value Observed: Strong upward trend from 1970 to 2025 Slight flattening around 1980 to 2000 Faster growth after 2000
  • The Weekly (Middle) component shows the effect of day of the week on prices X-axis shows the days Y-axis shows the small effect on price Observed: Slight dip on Monday Increase towards weekend (Saturday highest) Day of week has little impact on gold prices
  • The Yearly (Bottom) component shows the effect of time of year X-axis shows the day of year (can look at the year in quarters for better understanding) Y-axis shows the seasonal effect Observed: Peaks around the middle of the year Dips towards the end and start of the year Some seasonal pattern but relatively small compared to the the trend.

All components are in log scale, so seasonal effects represent proportional changes rather than absolute price differences Overall, the trend is the dominant factor driving gold prices.

5.3 Converting back to original scale

tail(gold_forecast[, c("ds", "yhat", "yhat_lower", "yhat_upper")])
##               ds     yhat yhat_lower yhat_upper
## 20872 2027-02-22 11.07818   10.93325   11.20737
## 20873 2027-02-23 11.07905   10.95442   11.20406
## 20874 2027-02-24 11.07995   10.95123   11.21959
## 20875 2027-02-25 11.08063   10.93989   11.20961
## 20876 2027-02-26 11.08147   10.95518   11.21386
## 20877 2027-02-27 11.08248   10.94237   11.22200
gold_forecast$price <- exp(gold_forecast$yhat)
gold_forecast$price_lower <- exp(gold_forecast$yhat_lower)
gold_forecast$price_upper <- exp(gold_forecast$yhat_upper)

tail(gold_forecast[, c("ds", "price", "price_lower", "price_upper")])
##               ds    price price_lower price_upper
## 20872 2027-02-22 64742.74    56008.04    73671.19
## 20873 2027-02-23 64799.10    57206.51    73428.04
## 20874 2027-02-24 64857.86    57024.01    74577.48
## 20875 2027-02-25 64901.64    56381.41    73836.88
## 20876 2027-02-26 64956.12    57249.77    74150.87
## 20877 2027-02-27 65021.88    56521.11    74757.39

Since the model was fitted on log transformed data, predictions are converted back to actual prices using the exponential function. This provides interpretable forecasts along with uncertainty ranges.

6. Model Comparisons

6.1 Model without seasonality

model_no_season <- prophet(prophet_data, yearly.seasonality = FALSE)
## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.
future2 <- make_future_dataframe(model_no_season, periods = 365)
forecast2 <- predict(model_no_season, future2)

plot(model_no_season, forecast2)

This model removes yearly seasonality to test its importance. The forecast remains largely unchanged, indicating that seasonal effects have minimal influence on gold prices. The graph looks very similar to the 1st grapgh of the closing gold prices

6.2 Model using recent data (from 2000)

recent_data <- prophet_data[prophet_data$ds > as.Date("2000-01-01"), ]

recent_model <- prophet(recent_data)
## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.
future_recent <- make_future_dataframe(recent_model, periods = 365)
forecast_recent <- predict(recent_model, future_recent)

plot(recent_model, forecast_recent)

This model uses only recent data to focus on modern market behaviour and understaning it. The forecast typically shows a steeper upward trend, reflecting stronger growth in recent years. This suggests that recent data may provide more relevant short term predictions.

7. Key Insights

  • Gold prices exhibit a strong long term upward trend
  • Growth accelerates significantly after the early 2000s
  • There are periods of high volatility linked to economic events
  • Seasonal effects are weak and do not significantly impact prices
  • Forecasts indicate continued growth with increasing uncertainty
  • Models based on recent data provide more realistic short term forecasts

8. Conclusion

This project shows us the application of Prophet for time series forecasting of gold prices. The results show that gold prices are primarily driven by long term trends rather than seasonal effects. While the model predicts continued growth, uncertainty increases over longer forecast timelines. Using recent data highlights stronger modern trends and provides more relevant forecasts for current market conditions.

9. Data Sources and further research

Data downloaded from Kaggle in csv format.The prices in this dataset are in USD ($). Further analysis can be done on the open prices and high and low prices recorded daily.
